item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my god. The ending. I can't see the rating module through the tears

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the css tip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The formatting issue can be solved by putting the following into the page, at any location (Although at the very top is the best practice):

 [[module CSS]] @media (max-width: 767px) { span { word-break: unset; } } [[/module]]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is spot on. The drawings he creates are representations of the original incident, as well as further trauma. I tried to drive it home with the second tab, where after the Foundation started restraining him, some of his drawings caused paralysis and straining muscles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if that may have actually been a suicide attempt, that the kid was in such pain that he tried to kill himself, which resulted in the anomalous abilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done. +1

I particularly appreciated the clever use of formatting so that in the later tabs, the reader doesn’t have to plow through the long-form version of the baseline article but can concentrate on seeing what the changes are.

Made it to +100! That’s super exciting!

If you liked this piece, check out my Author Page for more of my stuff!

A few of my personal favorites: SCP-4170 is another emotional one, though not nearly as tragic. SCP-4163 is a fun twist on the “harmless entity turns out to be dangerous” formula, and is probably my personal favorite. SCP-4357 is an outright comedy, and I’m very proud of the dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was so sure the package was going to have something horrible in it. Unexpectedly wholesome twist!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback! The last tab serves to explain why the Chaos Insurgency would deploy a powerful weapon against some random suburban house, as well as highlight a personal connection to the scp. I understand your criticism of this characterization of the EC, though, and I’ll think about that more for future articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ouch, that last amendment hit me really hard - I was wondering why the terrorists attacked that house specifically, and it was a very powerful way to resolve that question. Excellent portrayal of the cold Foundation with the medical solutions to an emotional problem too, and a respectful take on PTSD and disability.

Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note the second experiment:

"Subject viewed the image for 2 minutes before experiencing hemorrhaging of the tear ducts. Subject was incapacitated by a severe headache for 4.5 hours. Upon recovery, subject described the experience as "like [his] head was trying to pop." Subject survived, with moderate vision loss."

I feel the article establishes fairly clearly that the kid is recreating the events of the Incident with his drawings. Not too big a stretch to say a child experiences an infohazard as extreme pressure in his head and has the (apparently correct) idea of relieving the pressure.

Overall, this skip is heartwarming and a perfect example of the Ethics Committee doing its job correctly, even if I do think it works better as an example of that in the absence of the last tab. Giving him the blanket has enough impact as an ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I think the article would be much stronger without the last addendum. The way the Ethics Committee is portrayed is great, but having one of the members be the mother (or at least related) takes a lot out of the EC's actions being both empathetic and competent, and instead feels like this only happened because there was someone in the background guiding them. I would much rather preferred if there was no direct connection between the SCP and the EC and they simply know how to treat a mentally undeveloped child humanely because they actually know how to do their job.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll admit after the last ECR I was sure something bad was gonna happen, but I was thankfully proven wrong. It's nice to have some wholesome (albeit tragic) content for a change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i wish to hug the child

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aww poor kid :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure whether I buy the official story that the boy did this to himself - it seem highly unlikely that an 11-year-old would know to do this or would have the resolve to do it. Given that this was an attack on a Foundation official's home while they were absent, I would suggest that the husband was also probably a Foundation official. I think it's at least plausible that husband, as a Foundation official might have known enough about the infohazard to perform the procedure (or instruct the boy to perform the procedure) before succumbing to its effects.

I agree with other commenters that the twist doesn't land as hard as the article seems to expect, but the initial set-up and execution, along with ethics back-and-forth, had already won an upvote from me so it's hardly an issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: now THAT was a good twist ending. maybe it's because i'm a newer member, but i certainly didn't see it coming. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably in the minority here, but I think the last tab was unnecessary. The twist of foundation researchers being too cold and detached to recognize a simpler and more humane solution to the problem was good enough for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last ethics committee section is an excellent wham moment, and it's nice to see them being both ethical and effective in the article proper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to see something in the final experiment log that reflects a transitional stage - although if they weren't testing for a while after he received the blanket it wouldn't show up admittedly. The change between the second and third revision feels a little bit abrupt and "easy".

Very good piece, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed that shortly after posting, but unfortunately I don't think there's a way to fix it with the native wikidot syntax. Oh well! Glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big fan of this! Quick note, viewing this on my iPhone in the Google Chrome browser, the middle column for he tables in Revisions 2 and 3 is super-compressed such that it looks something like below. Not sure how to fix it, but just wanted to give you the heads up in case you did!

O
b
s
e
r
v
e
r
s

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't able to predict where it was going, and the twist was a complete surprise. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your feedback! The original ending was very different. Instead of an ethics committee member, it was an O5, and I played off the angle of O5’s being forbidden from interacting with SCPs. It was a long, drawn out dialogue that reviewers found to be a bit too far on the melodramatic side, so I cut it down and made it much more clinical.

Do you have any thoughts on a way to make the twist hit a bit harder? Even if it’s not something I integrate into this piece, I always love to do a little light workshopping!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one didn't do much for me. The story behind the skip is decent, but it was pretty easy to predict where this article will go after the first 2 documents. Foundation did the right thing and it worked, so doing ethical things may benefit everyone. This is understandable and was certainly explored before.

I feel like here this topic was presented too directly to make a significant impact. The final twist didn't hit that hard, either. I guess because it was a little too brief and there wasn't enough build-up for me to feel it.

But this is a clever concept and the piece is very well-written overall. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor kid. Poor Ethics member. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I've been trying to focus on writing tight, character-oriented stories lately, rather than venturing into more high-stakes XK territory. The Ethics Committee is also one of my favorite bits of site lore, and I hope to explore more about the interactions between the O5 Council and the Ethics Committee more in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this "tab of files" format to show internal bureaucratic revisions. The use of gray text for unchanged segments was also cool.

As a narrative I think it did a good job showing early containment difficulties, interwoven with the struggles of a kid put in a very difficult situation. The use of the item and final collapsible were enjoyable ways to cap it off, without it being too deus ex machina-y.

Also I'm a sucker for good content featuring the Ethics Committee.

+1

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded: Many thanks to tupacofficial and Pedantique for their critique in the forums!
